name: Toyol
alias: the spirit child · the little thief
body: A Toyol is a small green-skinned infant spirit, eyes wide and red, raised from a dead fetus by a bomoh and kept in a jar or an old urn. Its keeper feeds it drops of blood from a pricked toe and sends it out at night to steal: money, gold, small valuables lifted from sleeping households who never feel a thing and wake only to a vague, unaccountable poverty.

It has the mind of a toddler, easily delighted and just as easily fooled, and that is its only weakness. A house that suspects a Toyol will leave out beads, marbles, or a scatter of sand by the door; the creature stops to count and play and forgets why it came, and is gone by morning with nothing. The same childishness that makes it a thief makes it harmless to anyone who treats it as the infant it is.

What lingers is the smallness of the menace, the idea that the thing emptying your purse is a baby that wanted to play, and that somewhere a person chose to raise it from a grave and feed it from their own body. In the older quarters a sudden run of bad luck around money still draws the half-joking accusation, and shopkeepers have been known to keep a needle stuck in the till or a saucer of beads near the cashbox, just in case the little thief comes counting.
note: Sudden, unexplained good fortune in a neighbour was the classic accusation: if their household prospered while yours quietly thinned, the suspicion was that a Toyol worked the streets between you. Money kept beside a mirror, a needle, or a few loose beads is said to repel it, the reflection and the small bright things being enough to break its concentration.

The bond between keeper and Toyol is a debt that does not die with the owner. When the keeper passes, the creature must be fed and passed on, or it turns on the surviving family, and that is how the jars travel down the years and across households. The tale is told throughout maritime Southeast Asia, in Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia, usually with a moral edge: wealth that arrives too fast and from nowhere is wealth someone paid for in a way best not asked about.
